title: Public Speaking Adaptive Exam
description: 100 single-choice Public Speaking questions. A normal round serves 25; the final exam serves all 100. Every distractor is a real course term or definition, so choose the best exact match.

questions.prompt: Which answer is the most precise exam meaning of "Three types of audience analysis"?

options.text: Audience analysis of the occasion and setting — audience size, physical environment, time, and the reason the audience has gathered.
Keeping the audience foremost in mind at every stage. The speaker's goal is to gain a desired response from listeners, not to show off — adapt the message to them.
In Stand Up, Speak Out: demographic, psychographic, and situational. Together they tell you who your listeners are, what they think, and the context you'll speak in.
Audience analysis based on attitudes, beliefs, and values (and opinions). Less verifiable than demographics but essential for persuasion. Values form the foundation that shapes beliefs and attitudes.

questions.explanation: Best match: Three types of audience analysis - In Stand Up, Speak Out: demographic, psychographic, and situational. Together they tell you who your listeners are, what they think, and the context you'll speak in. The other choices are real Public Speaking terms or definitions (Situational analysis, Audience-centeredness, Psychographic analysis), but they answer a neighboring concept rather than this exact prompt. Watch the general word(s): analysis = analysis means a named inspection checklist, not just thinking.
memoryFocus: analysis means a named inspection checklist, not just thinking

questions.prompt: Which answer is the most precise exam meaning of "Audience disposition (key factor)"?

options.text: Adjusting topic, language, examples, and delivery to fit the specific audience — both while preparing and in real time, by reading audience feedback as you speak.

Gathering and analyzing information about your listeners — their demographics, attitudes, and situation — so you can adapt the speech to their knowledge, interests, and needs. The cornerstone of effective speaking.

What you most need to gauge varies by audience: an audience's existing knowledge of the topic often matters most when informing (e.g., a talk to expectant parents). Match depth and vocabulary to it. (Exam scenario.)
Creating common ground with the audience by stressing shared values, experiences, and goals — making listeners feel "this speaker is like me."

questions.prompt: Which answer is the most precise exam meaning of "Value"?

options.text: Audience analysis based on group memberships — age, gender, race/ethnicity, religion, education, socioeconomic status, group affiliations. The most straightforward and verifiable of the three types.
Creating common ground with the audience by stressing shared values, experiences, and goals — making listeners feel "this speaker is like me."

questions.prompt: Which answer is the most precise exam meaning of "Extemporaneous delivery"?

options.text: Delivering a speech from memory, word-for-word. Risks sounding mechanical and the danger of forgetting passages.
Speaking from a brief keyword outline after thorough preparation and practice. The most versatile, natural, recommended method — allows real audience contact. (Exam topic.)
Communication through the body: eye contact, facial expressions, gestures, posture, movement, and appearance.
Objects/models, photographs, diagrams, charts and graphs (line, bar, pie), maps, video, and the speaker themselves. Pick the type that best clarifies the idea.
